permissions.commentary: LGPLv2.1 contains no explicit patent grant from contributors. Section 11 (patent-tension clause, inherited from GPLv2) requires that if patent obligations contradict the license you must not distribute the Library at all, but no express patent license is granted to downstream recipients. This omission is one motivation for LGPLv3, which inherits GPLv3's explicit patent provisions by reference.

b) You must cause the files modified to carry prominent notices stating that you changed the files and the date of any change
Section 4: distribute the Library ... in object code or executable form ... provided that you accompany it with the complete corresponding machine-readable source code
Section 6(a): Accompany the work with the complete corresponding machine-readable source code for the Library including whatever changes were used in the work
Section 6(c): Accompany the work with a written offer, valid for at least three years, to give the same user the materials specified in Subsection 6a

conditions.commentary: LGPLv2.1 obligations attach to distribution of copies. Providing network access to software that links the Library is not distribution and does not trigger source-disclosure. No AGPL-style network-use provision exists.

citations.note: a) The modified work must itself be a software library
c) You must cause the whole of the work to be licensed at no charge to all third parties under the terms of this License
Section 5: A program that contains no derivative of any portion of the Library, but is designed to work with the Library by being compiled or linked with it, is called a 'work that uses the Library'
Such a work, in isolation, is not a derivative work of the Library, and therefore falls outside the scope of this License
Section 6: As an exception to the Sections above, you may also combine or link a 'work that uses the Library' with the Library ... and distribute that work under terms of your choice

conditions.commentary: LGPLv2.1 is weak copyleft and the same-license requirement has a narrow footprint that is easy to misstate. Modifications to the Library itself (works based on the Library under Section 2) must be licensed under the LGPL and must themselves be libraries (Sections 2a and 2c). But a 'work that uses the Library' (application code that links against the Library without incorporating it) is explicitly outside the License's scope (Sections 5-6) and may be distributed under any terms, provided the user can replace or relink the Library (typically via shared libraries or by supplying object files of the application). The Section 6 conditions, not same-licensing, govern the combined distribution.
